_comment: Construct definitions for Project Majdal. Population, environment, reading level, and response format come from scale_defaults in pipeline_settings.json unless explicitly overridden here on a per-scale basis.

scales.construct_definition: The tendency to seek out, appreciate, and engage with varied experience, aesthetic impressions, imaginative ideas, and intellectual pursuits. Individuals high in Openness are curious, imaginative, and receptive to novel and unconventional perspectives. They enjoy exploring abstract ideas, appreciate art and beauty across forms, notice and reflect on their own inner feelings, and are willing to question established values and authority. Those low in Openness are more conventional in outlook, prefer the familiar to the novel, are less drawn to aesthetic or intellectual pursuits, and tend to hold traditional values and defer to established ways of doing things.
scales.high_anchor: Seeks out new experiences, ideas, and aesthetic impressions. Engages with art, literature, and abstract thinking. Attends to inner feelings and imaginative life. Is willing to question conventional beliefs and authority. Prefers variety and novelty over routine.
scales.low_anchor: Prefers the familiar, conventional, and practical over the novel and abstract. Has little interest in art, aesthetics, or theoretical ideas. Is less attentive to inner experience and imagination. Defers to tradition and established authority. Prefers routine over variety.
scales.discriminant_description: Openness to Experience concerns receptivity to varied experience, aesthetic sensitivity, and intellectual curiosity. It is distinct from Conscientiousness, which concerns self-discipline and order rather than curiosity or imagination. It is also distinct from Extraversion, which concerns social engagement and positive affect rather than aesthetic or intellectual receptivity. Openness is not the same as intelligence or cognitive ability — it reflects orientation toward experience, not capacity.
scales.reference: Costa, P. T., & McCrae, R. R. (1992). Revised NEO Personality Inventory (NEO-PI-R) and NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) professional manual. Psychological Assessment Resources. See also John, O. P., & Srivastava, S. (1999). The Big Five trait taxonomy. In L. A. Pervin & O. P. John (Eds.), Handbook of personality (2nd ed., pp. 102-138). Guilford Press.

scales.construct_definition: The tendency to be organised, diligent, self-disciplined, and deliberate in pursuing goals and meeting obligations. Individuals high in Conscientiousness work carefully and systematically, plan ahead, follow through on commitments, maintain order in their environment and activities, and exercise impulse control in the service of long-term goals. They are dependable, hardworking, and achievement-oriented, and feel discomfort when tasks are incomplete or done carelessly. Those low in Conscientiousness are less organised and systematic, more impulsive and spontaneous in decision-making, less concerned with precision or thoroughness, and more willing to leave tasks incomplete or proceed without a clear plan.
scales.high_anchor: Works in an organised, systematic, and thorough manner. Plans ahead and follows through on commitments reliably. Exercises self-discipline and impulse control. Strives for high standards of accuracy and completeness in work. Maintains order in environment and schedule.
scales.low_anchor: Approaches tasks in an unsystematic or disorganised way. Acts impulsively without careful planning. Is tolerant of incomplete or imprecise work. Struggles with self-discipline and follow-through. Is comfortable with disorder in environment and schedule.
scales.discriminant_description: Conscientiousness concerns self-regulation, organisation, and goal-directed diligence. It is distinct from Agreeableness, which concerns interpersonal warmth and cooperation rather than self-discipline. It is also distinct from Neuroticism — a conscientious person is not necessarily anxious or guilt-prone, though both traits can involve concern about performance. Conscientiousness is about regulated pursuit of goals, not about the emotional experience of pursuing them.
scales.reference: Costa, P. T., & McCrae, R. R. (1992). Revised NEO Personality Inventory (NEO-PI-R) and NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) professional manual. Psychological Assessment Resources.

scales.construct_definition: The tendency to be outgoing, energetic, assertive, and positive in social and emotional life. Individuals high in Extraversion enjoy being around others, seek out stimulation and excitement, experience positive emotions readily, and are inclined to take social initiative and assert themselves in groups. They are talkative, enthusiastic, and drawn to lively environments. Those low in Extraversion (introverts) prefer quieter, less stimulating environments, are more reserved in social settings, experience positive affect less intensely, and are less inclined to seek out attention or dominance. Introversion is not the same as shyness or social anxiety — it reflects a lower preference for stimulation and social engagement, not a fear of it.
scales.high_anchor: Enjoys and seeks out social interaction and stimulating environments. Is talkative, enthusiastic, and expressive of positive emotion. Takes initiative in social and group settings. Feels energised by being around other people. Is drawn to excitement and activity.
scales.low_anchor: Prefers quieter, less stimulating environments and smaller social groups. Is reserved, soft-spoken, and less expressive of emotion. Feels drained rather than energised by extended social interaction. Does not seek out attention, excitement, or social dominance.
scales.discriminant_description: Extraversion concerns sociability, positive affect, and activity level. It is distinct from Agreeableness, which concerns warmth and cooperation toward others rather than social engagement per se — one can be warm and agreeable while still being introverted. It is also distinct from Openness, which concerns receptivity to varied experience. Low Extraversion is not the same as anxiety or social phobia (which belong to Neuroticism) — introverted individuals are not necessarily anxious in social settings, simply less drawn to them.
scales.reference: Costa, P. T., & McCrae, R. R. (1992). Revised NEO Personality Inventory (NEO-PI-R) and NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) professional manual. Psychological Assessment Resources.

scales.construct_definition: The tendency to be cooperative, trusting, warm, and considerate in interpersonal relationships. Individuals high in Agreeableness are sympathetic to others, willing to help, inclined to trust people's good intentions, and prefer cooperation over competition or conflict. They are tender-minded, modest about themselves, and straightforward rather than manipulative in their dealings. Those low in Agreeableness are more competitive, sceptical of others' motives, willing to express anger or criticism, and more focused on their own interests in interpersonal situations. They may be blunt, tough-minded, or antagonistic.
scales.high_anchor: Is trusting, cooperative, and considerate toward others. Is sympathetic to others' needs and willing to help. Is modest about own achievements and straightforward in dealings with others. Prefers harmony and avoids unnecessary conflict.
scales.low_anchor: Is sceptical of others' motives and competitive rather than cooperative. Is willing to express criticism, anger, or antagonism. Is focused on own interests in interpersonal situations. Is tough-minded and willing to engage in conflict.
scales.discriminant_description: Agreeableness concerns warmth, trust, and cooperation in interpersonal relationships. It is distinct from Extraversion, which concerns social engagement and positive affect rather than the quality of interpersonal orientation — an extraverted person can be disagreeable (outgoing but combative), and an introverted person can be highly agreeable (quietly warm and cooperative). It is also distinct from Conscientiousness; reliability in meeting commitments is a Conscientiousness property, whereas warmth and cooperation in how one meets them is Agreeableness.
scales.reference: Costa, P. T., & McCrae, R. R. (1992). Revised NEO Personality Inventory (NEO-PI-R) and NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) professional manual. Psychological Assessment Resources.

scales.construct_definition: The tendency to experience negative emotions — including anxiety, sadness, anger, guilt, and self-consciousness — readily and intensely, and to perceive ordinary situations as threatening or distressing. Individuals high in Neuroticism worry frequently, feel tense and vulnerable, are prone to low mood and emotional reactivity, and may struggle to cope with stress and return to emotional equilibrium after setbacks. Those low in Neuroticism (high in Emotional Stability) are calm, relaxed, resilient under stress, and less prone to experiencing strong negative emotions. They recover quickly from setbacks and are less affected by daily frustrations and worries.
scales.high_anchor: Worries frequently and feels anxious in ordinary situations. Experiences negative emotions — sadness, anger, guilt, self-consciousness — readily and intensely. Feels vulnerable to stress and struggles to recover from setbacks. Is emotionally reactive to daily events.
scales.low_anchor: Is calm, relaxed, and emotionally steady. Experiences negative emotions less frequently and less intensely. Recovers quickly from setbacks and stress. Is not easily upset by daily frustrations or worries. Feels secure and resilient.
scales.discriminant_description: Neuroticism concerns the frequency and intensity of negative emotional experience and vulnerability to stress. It is distinct from low Agreeableness — a person can be emotionally stable yet disagreeable (calmly hostile), or emotionally reactive yet agreeable (anxious but warm). It is also distinct from low Extraversion; introversion is a preference for less stimulation, whereas Neuroticism is a propensity to experience distress. Neuroticism is not the same as clinical depression or anxiety disorder — it reflects a dimension of normal personality variation, not a diagnostic category.
scales.reference: Costa, P. T., & McCrae, R. R. (1992). Revised NEO Personality Inventory (NEO-PI-R) and NEO Five-Factor Inventory (NEO-FFI) professional manual. Psychological Assessment Resources.
